question_text: In the WACC formula, why is the cost of debt multiplied by (1 - tax rate)?

options.text: To account for the risk of default
To reflect the tax deductibility of interest expense
To convert the nominal rate to a real rate
To adjust for the seniority of debt in the capital structure

correct_answer: To reflect the tax deductibility of interest expense
explanation: Interest payments on debt are tax-deductible, meaning they reduce a company's taxable income. If a company pays 6% interest and has a 25% tax rate, the after-tax cost of that debt is only 4.5% (6% x (1 - 0.25)). The (1 - tax rate) factor in WACC captures this tax shield benefit, which is one of the key advantages debt has over equity financing. This is why, up to a point, adding debt to a company's capital structure can lower its overall cost of capital.
